texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Brine disposal is becoming a huge problem as a consequence of the extensive application of reverse osmosis (RO) technology for desalination purposes. According to Fritzmann et al. [1], the capacity of RO desalination industry today is around 20 million m 3 /day; the water recovery in seawater RO operations generally varies from 30to50%,beingtheremainingpartrejected. Recent studies in literature confirmed the impact of discharged RO concentrates on soil, on groundwater (contamination by residual chlorine, heavy metals [2]), and on marine fauna and flora (increase of mortality of PosidoniaOceanica [3], damageofbenthicorganismsduetocoagulants[4], etc.). In this context, innovative techniques and approaches aiming to reduce the volume of wastes and to extract dissolved salts from RO reject brines can represent an interesting opportunity towards sustainability, also in terms of cost-saving [5].
texts.text: Brine disposal is becoming a huge problem as a consequence of the extensive application of reverse osmosis (RO) technology for desalination purposes. According to Fritzmann et al. [1], the capacity of RO desalination industry today is around 20 million m 3 /day; the water recovery in seawater RO operations generally varies from 30to50%,beingtheremainingpartrejected. Recent studies in literature confirmed the impact of discharged RO concentrates on soil, on groundwater (contamination by residual chlorine, heavy metals [2]), and on marine fauna and flora (increase of mortality of PosidoniaOceanica [3], damageofbenthicorganismsduetocoagulants[4], etc.). In this context, innovative techniques and approaches aiming to reduce the volume of wastes and to extract dissolved salts from RO reject brines can represent an interesting opportunity towards sustainability, also in terms of cost-saving [5].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work aims at investigating the performance, in terms of water recovery and NaCl crystallization kinetics, of a membrane distillation-crystallization (MDC) bench-scale plant operated on brines discharged from a seawater reverse osmosis (RO) unit.
texts.text: This work aims at investigating the performance, in terms of water recovery and NaCl crystallization kinetics, of a membrane distillation-crystallization (MDC) bench-scale plant operated on brines discharged from a seawater reverse osmosis (RO) unit.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experimental tests carried out on artificial RO concentrates resulted in the production of 21 kg/m 3 of NaCl crystals, exhibiting substantially a ordinary cubic shape with size between 20 and 200 𝛍 m; the final water recovery factor increased up to 90%. Analogous investigations carried out on RO brines from natural seawater were affected by the presence of dissolved organic matter, showing a 20% reduction of the amount of salt crystallized, and a 8% decrease of the transmembrane flux.
texts.text: Experimental tests carried out on artificial RO concentrates resulted in the production of 21 kg/m 3 of NaCl crystals, exhibiting substantially a ordinary cubic shape with size between 20 and 200 𝛍 m; the final water recovery factor increased up to 90%. Analogous investigations carried out on RO brines from natural seawater were affected by the presence of dissolved organic matter, showing a 20% reduction of the amount of salt crystallized, and a 8% decrease of the transmembrane flux.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Growth rate of sodium chloride crystals generated from natural RO brines varied between 0.8 × 10 -8 and 2.8 × 10 -8 m/s; these values were 15-23% lower than those measured for NaCl crystals grown form artificial concentrates. In general, the NaCl crystal size distribution was characterized by a narrow dispersion (coefficient of variation within 35-40%).
texts.text: Growth rate of sodium chloride crystals generated from natural RO brines varied between 0.8 × 10 -8 and 2.8 × 10 -8 m/s; these values were 15-23% lower than those measured for NaCl crystals grown form artificial concentrates. In general, the NaCl crystal size distribution was characterized by a narrow dispersion (coefficient of variation within 35-40%).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MDC operations were stable over 100h as a result of a careful control of supersaturation, polarization phenomena, nucleation process and hydrodynamics.
texts.text: MDC operations were stable over 100h as a result of a careful control of supersaturation, polarization phenomena, nucleation process and hydrodynamics.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: valuable salts from nanofiltration brines produced by desalination operations [6]. MDC exploits the excellent ability of membrane distillation (MD) process, a thermally driven operation to concentrate aqueous solutions up to supersaturation. MD can operate with reasonable fluxes under moderate temperature gradients at high solution concentrations, while reverse osmosis fails as a consequence of the suppression of driving force caused by the increase of osmotic pressure [7].
texts.text: valuable salts from nanofiltration brines produced by desalination operations [6]. MDC exploits the excellent ability of membrane distillation (MD) process, a thermally driven operation to concentrate aqueous solutions up to supersaturation. MD can operate with reasonable fluxes under moderate temperature gradients at high solution concentrations, while reverse osmosis fails as a consequence of the suppression of driving force caused by the increase of osmotic pressure [7].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In MDC, the high contact area provided by hollow fiber membranes allows to achieve reliable evaporation fluxes already at moderate temperatures (40-50 ◦ C) with energy consumption of about 15-20kWh/m 3 [8]. On the other hand, conventional evaporative equipments for NaCl crystallization, i.e. forced circulation crystallizers and draft-tube-baffled crystallizers, are generally operated at temperature higher than 70 ◦ C, with specific energy consumption of ∼ 30kWh/m 3 of distillate [9].
texts.text: In MDC, the high contact area provided by hollow fiber membranes allows to achieve reliable evaporation fluxes already at moderate temperatures (40-50 ◦ C) with energy consumption of about 15-20kWh/m 3 [8]. On the other hand, conventional evaporative equipments for NaCl crystallization, i.e. forced circulation crystallizers and draft-tube-baffled crystallizers, are generally operated at temperature higher than 70 ◦ C, with specific energy consumption of ∼ 30kWh/m 3 of distillate [9].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The application of MDC to the crystallization of sodium chloride has been firstly addressed by Curcio et al. [10] by operating at moderate temperature gradient (feed and distillate inlet temperatures of 29 ◦ C and 9 ◦ C, respectively) in order to keep low the transmembrane flux ( ∼ 1.1 × 10 -4 kg/m 2 s), so reducing the influence of the concentration polarization on the supersaturation profile, and preventing eventual incrustation at the membrane surface.
texts.text: The application of MDC to the crystallization of sodium chloride has been firstly addressed by Curcio et al. [10] by operating at moderate temperature gradient (feed and distillate inlet temperatures of 29 ◦ C and 9 ◦ C, respectively) in order to keep low the transmembrane flux ( ∼ 1.1 × 10 -4 kg/m 2 s), so reducing the influence of the concentration polarization on the supersaturation profile, and preventing eventual incrustation at the membrane surface.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The integration of membrane distillation with crystallization unit has been operated by Gryta [11] to produce 100 kg m -2 d -1 of NaCl crystals. Anhydrous sodium sulphate crystals with a relatively narrow crystal size distribution have been obtained at high feed temperatures (50-60 ◦ C) with fluxes up to 20 l m -2 h -1 byTunetal. [12]; it was also found that, due to a high evaporation rate and relevant polarization phenomena, the transmembrane flux gradually declined. NaCl crystals from aqueous solutions showing a coefficient of variation within 25-35% and an average size of 43 𝛍 mhave been obtained by Konig and Weckesser [13].
texts.text: The integration of membrane distillation with crystallization unit has been operated by Gryta [11] to produce 100 kg m -2 d -1 of NaCl crystals. Anhydrous sodium sulphate crystals with a relatively narrow crystal size distribution have been obtained at high feed temperatures (50-60 ◦ C) with fluxes up to 20 l m -2 h -1 byTunetal. [12]; it was also found that, due to a high evaporation rate and relevant polarization phenomena, the transmembrane flux gradually declined. NaCl crystals from aqueous solutions showing a coefficient of variation within 25-35% and an average size of 43 𝛍 mhave been obtained by Konig and Weckesser [13].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our experimental work investigates the crystallization kinetics of sodium chloride (NaCl) from RO brines by MDC, as essential requisite to optimize the membrane-assisted crystallization process in terms of productivity, controlled size and shape distribution. The empirical approach is based on a transient crystal size distribution (CSD) data acquired by image analysis, and an experimental measurements of solution concentration and suspension density curves during batch crystallization tests. The proposed comparison between artificial (without organic matter) and natural RO concentrates aims at quantifying the effect of organic matter dissolved in raw seawater on the distillate flux, suspension density, and NaCl nucleation and growth rates.
texts.text: Our experimental work investigates the crystallization kinetics of sodium chloride (NaCl) from RO brines by MDC, as essential requisite to optimize the membrane-assisted crystallization process in terms of productivity, controlled size and shape distribution. The empirical approach is based on a transient crystal size distribution (CSD) data acquired by image analysis, and an experimental measurements of solution concentration and suspension density curves during batch crystallization tests. The proposed comparison between artificial (without organic matter) and natural RO concentrates aims at quantifying the effect of organic matter dissolved in raw seawater on the distillate flux, suspension density, and NaCl nucleation and growth rates.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Materials and methods
texts.text: 3. Materials and methods

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Preparation and characterization of feed solutions
texts.text: 3.1. Preparation and characterization of feed solutions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Raw seawater was collected from Tyrrenian Sea beside the coastal city of Amantea (Calabria), and then pre-filtered through 0.5 𝛍 m cartridge filters. Reverse Osmosis concentrates were obtained by processing seawater in a RO lab-scale unit MATRIX (Matrix desalination Inc.) equipped with 3 modules OSMONICS AD 2540FF2.5in.,andoperatedat800psiand25 ◦ Cincompleterecycle of retentate until reaching a global recovery factor of 30%. The solution composition of both raw water and RO concentrated natural seawater was determined by ion chromatographer 861 Advanced Compact IC (Metrohm AG, Switzerland) equipped with METROSEP C2-150 columns for cations and anions analysis. The total organic carbon(TOC)contentwasevaluatedbyTOC-VCSN TotalOrganicCarbon Analyzer (Shimadzu, Japan). Synthetic RO concentrates were prepared according to the ionic composition reported in Table 1 by dissolving reagent grade NaCl (Carlo Erba 368256), CaCl2 · 2H2O (Riedel-deHaen 31307), MgCl2 (Carlo Erba 349354), NaHCO3 (Fluka 88208), Na2CO3 (Fluka 71350), Na2SO4 (Carlo Erba 483007), and KCl (Carlo Erba 471177) in ultrapure water (Purelab, ELGA) without

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Membrane distillation-crystallization
texts.text: 3.2. Membrane distillation-crystallization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The MDC bench-scale experimental setup used in this work is shown in Fig. 1 (block diagram in [17]). It consists of: (A) a homemade membrane module assembling Accurel PP polypropylene hollow-fibers with a total membrane area of 0.4m 2 ; (B) a gear pumpSERIE N (Pompe Cucchi, Italy) automated by AC TECH Intelligent Drive, recirculating the concentrate solution; (C) a centrifugal pump BGM5/A (Lowara, Italy) recirculating the solvent (water) that permeates through the membrane; (D) a heater Mod. 112A (VWR, USA) that increases the solution temperature up to 40 ◦ C at the entrance of the membrane module; (E) five SPER SCIENTIFIC 800012 Pt multi-channel thermocouples ( T ) with sensitivity ± 0.1 ◦ C monitoring temperatures at the inlet and outlet of the membrane module (on both permeate and concentrate sides); (F) an industrial balance with HDWE RS485 indicator connected to the distillate tank to estimate the transmembrane flux of water; (G) twoF14flowmeters(Blue-WhiteIndustries,Ltd.)operatingonboth permeate and concentrate lines. The temperatures in the distillate reservoir (H) and jacketed retentate/crystallization tank (I) were maintained at 20 ◦ C and 30 ◦ C, respectively, by thermostatic baths Digital Plus RTE201 (Neslab, USA). The initial volume V 0 of brine chargedtothemembranedistillation-crystallizationplantwas50l; batch operations were protracted for 80-90 h.
texts.text: The MDC bench-scale experimental setup used in this work is shown in Fig. 1 (block diagram in [17]). It consists of: (A) a homemade membrane module assembling Accurel PP polypropylene hollow-fibers with a total membrane area of 0.4m 2 ; (B) a gear pumpSERIE N (Pompe Cucchi, Italy) automated by AC TECH Intelligent Drive, recirculating the concentrate solution; (C) a centrifugal pump BGM5/A (Lowara, Italy) recirculating the solvent (water) that permeates through the membrane; (D) a heater Mod. 112A (VWR, USA) that increases the solution temperature up to 40 ◦ C at the entrance of the membrane module; (E) five SPER SCIENTIFIC 800012 Pt multi-channel thermocouples ( T ) with sensitivity ± 0.1 ◦ C monitoring temperatures at the inlet and outlet of the membrane module (on both permeate and concentrate sides); (F) an industrial balance with HDWE RS485 indicator connected to the distillate tank to estimate the transmembrane flux of water; (G) twoF14flowmeters(Blue-WhiteIndustries,Ltd.)operatingonboth permeate and concentrate lines. The temperatures in the distillate reservoir (H) and jacketed retentate/crystallization tank (I) were maintained at 20 ◦ C and 30 ◦ C, respectively, by thermostatic baths Digital Plus RTE201 (Neslab, USA). The initial volume V 0 of brine chargedtothemembranedistillation-crystallizationplantwas50l; batch operations were protracted for 80-90 h.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Results and discussion
texts.text: 4. Results and discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Transmembrane fluxes
texts.text: 4.1. Transmembrane fluxes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 2 illustrates the time-dependent water flux and NaCl concentration profiles registered during membrane distillationcrystallization batch tests carried out at moderate temperature gradient ( /Delta1 T ≈ 20 ◦ C), that resulted in a initial transmembrane flux of ∼ 4 × 10 -4 kgm -2 s -1 . The subsequent progressive flux decline ( ∼ 30% at the end of each test) was caused by the vapour pressure decrease due to the increase in solute concentration. The achievement of NaCl supersaturation (reached after 81 and 92h when treating natural and synthetic RO retentates, respectively) did not result in a significant flux decrease. Therefore, crystallization tests were carried out under almost constant transmembrane fluxes of 3.1 × 10 -4 and 2.7 × 10 -4 kgm -2 s -1 for natural and synthetic brines, respectively. These findings confirmed the good countermeasures (appropriate control of evaporation rate and hydrodynamics)takenagainstencrustationanddepositofparticles on the membrane surface that caused drastic reduction of permeability noticed in Tun et al. [12].
texts.text: Fig. 2 illustrates the time-dependent water flux and NaCl concentration profiles registered during membrane distillationcrystallization batch tests carried out at moderate temperature gradient ( /Delta1 T ≈ 20 ◦ C), that resulted in a initial transmembrane flux of ∼ 4 × 10 -4 kgm -2 s -1 . The subsequent progressive flux decline ( ∼ 30% at the end of each test) was caused by the vapour pressure decrease due to the increase in solute concentration. The achievement of NaCl supersaturation (reached after 81 and 92h when treating natural and synthetic RO retentates, respectively) did not result in a significant flux decrease. Therefore, crystallization tests were carried out under almost constant transmembrane fluxes of 3.1 × 10 -4 and 2.7 × 10 -4 kgm -2 s -1 for natural and synthetic brines, respectively. These findings confirmed the good countermeasures (appropriate control of evaporation rate and hydrodynamics)takenagainstencrustationanddepositofparticles on the membrane surface that caused drastic reduction of permeability noticed in Tun et al. [12].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Batch crystallization processes are characterized by a supersaturation profile changing with time. In MDC, the persistence of a significant distillate flux was able to sustain the supersaturation ratio S (that progressively increased from 1 to 1.15 during experiments) against its reduction caused by the formation of NaCl crystals leaving the mother liquor.
texts.text: Batch crystallization processes are characterized by a supersaturation profile changing with time. In MDC, the persistence of a significant distillate flux was able to sustain the supersaturation ratio S (that progressively increased from 1 to 1.15 during experiments) against its reduction caused by the formation of NaCl crystals leaving the mother liquor.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When treating RO brines from natural seawater, the observed average reduction of the distillate flux with respect to artificial retentates was 8%, that became more prominent ( -13%) at NaCl concentration approaching the solubility limit. These observations agree with the expectation of a deterioration of MDC performance as a consequence of the membrane fouling due to the presence of natural organic matter (NOM) in the feed seawater [18].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The possibility to obtain simultaneously pure water (the hydrophobicnatureofthemembranepreventthetransportofnonvolatile solutes from feed to permeate side) and crystalline product is an attractive feature of MDC process. At the end of each test, the total water recovery factor achieved was of 88-89%: starting from 50l of solution, the amount of pure water recovered by distillation was 44-44.5l, with conductivity ranging from 0.55 𝛍 S/cm to 3.5 𝛍 S/cm. Experimental data confirm the potential of this innovative approach for drinking water production in the logic of zero liquid discharge.
texts.text: The possibility to obtain simultaneously pure water (the hydrophobicnatureofthemembranepreventthetransportofnonvolatile solutes from feed to permeate side) and crystalline product is an attractive feature of MDC process. At the end of each test, the total water recovery factor achieved was of 88-89%: starting from 50l of solution, the amount of pure water recovered by distillation was 44-44.5l, with conductivity ranging from 0.55 𝛍 S/cm to 3.5 𝛍 S/cm. Experimental data confirm the potential of this innovative approach for drinking water production in the logic of zero liquid discharge.

texts.orig: 4.2. Polarization phenomena
texts.text: 4.2. Polarization phenomena

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polarization phenomena occurring at the membrane surface might affect the supersaturation profile along the membrane module. In particular, concentration polarization refers to the increase of solute concentration ( cs ) at the membrane interface (wall) with respect to its bulk value as a consequence of the solvent evaporation. According to the film model:
texts.text: Polarization phenomena occurring at the membrane surface might affect the supersaturation profile along the membrane module. In particular, concentration polarization refers to the increase of solute concentration ( cs ) at the membrane interface (wall) with respect to its bulk value as a consequence of the solvent evaporation. According to the film model:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: CPC(concentrationpolarizationcoefficient) = c s,wall c s,bulk = exp ( J /SUBk ) (8.a)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where J is the transmembrane flux, /SUB the solution density and k is the mass transfer coefficient, estimated in laminar regime using the Leveque equation [20]:
texts.text: where J is the transmembrane flux, /SUB the solution density and k is the mass transfer coefficient, estimated in laminar regime using the Leveque equation [20]:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Eq. (8.b), d is the fiber inner diameter, u the solution velocity, l the fiber length and D the diffusion coefficient. Our strategy, devoted to minimize this effect by keeping the flux low, resulted in CPC values varying between 1.008 and 1.014. As a term of comparison, Tun et al. [12] - who observed the complete suppression of permeability due to severe clogging of the membrane surface due to crystals formed - have reported an initial CPC value of 1.2-1.3 (resulting from initial permeation rates of 10-20 kg m 2 h -1 ) that decreased gradually during the batch run as the transmembrane flux dropped. The concomitant effect of temperature polarization on NaCl supersaturation was considered negligible due to the insensitive behaviour of sodium chloride solubility with temperature: +0.2% every 10 ◦ C [21].
texts.text: In Eq. (8.b), d is the fiber inner diameter, u the solution velocity, l the fiber length and D the diffusion coefficient. Our strategy, devoted to minimize this effect by keeping the flux low, resulted in CPC values varying between 1.008 and 1.014. As a term of comparison, Tun et al. [12] - who observed the complete suppression of permeability due to severe clogging of the membrane surface due to crystals formed - have reported an initial CPC value of 1.2-1.3 (resulting from initial permeation rates of 10-20 kg m 2 h -1 ) that decreased gradually during the batch run as the transmembrane flux dropped. The concomitant effect of temperature polarization on NaCl supersaturation was considered negligible due to the insensitive behaviour of sodium chloride solubility with temperature: +0.2% every 10 ◦ C [21].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. NaCl crystallization kinetics
texts.text: 4.3. NaCl crystallization kinetics

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The increase of suspension density during batch MDC tests is reported in Fig. 3. The yield of NaCl crystals approaches 21 kg per cubic meter of RO artificial brine after 3 h since the achievement of supersaturation. A lower amount of solids ( -20%) was produced from natural RO brines, thus evidencing the negative impact on the crystallization kinetics of organic matter dissolved in natural seawater.
texts.text: The increase of suspension density during batch MDC tests is reported in Fig. 3. The yield of NaCl crystals approaches 21 kg per cubic meter of RO artificial brine after 3 h since the achievement of supersaturation. A lower amount of solids ( -20%) was produced from natural RO brines, thus evidencing the negative impact on the crystallization kinetics of organic matter dissolved in natural seawater.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The presence of NaCl crystals on the membrane surface confirms that the polymeric surface acts as promoter of heterogeneous nucleation. As shown in Fig. 4, crystals of sodium chloride nucleating at the membrane surface showed the ordinary cubic shape; some exception concerned the moderate morphological changes (slight elongation) most probably due to the presence of different ions in the RO brines acting as impurities. The size of detected crystals typically varied between 20 and 200 𝛍 m, showing reduced values ( -10% c.a.) when NaCl crystals grew from natural seawater RO concentrates.
texts.text: The presence of NaCl crystals on the membrane surface confirms that the polymeric surface acts as promoter of heterogeneous nucleation. As shown in Fig. 4, crystals of sodium chloride nucleating at the membrane surface showed the ordinary cubic shape; some exception concerned the moderate morphological changes (slight elongation) most probably due to the presence of different ions in the RO brines acting as impurities. The size of detected crystals typically varied between 20 and 200 𝛍 m, showing reduced values ( -10% c.a.) when NaCl crystals grew from natural seawater RO concentrates.

The NaCl growth rate order g showed a substantial independence from the low organic content present in natural seawater ( g =0.56, lower by 7% with respect to value found for artificial RO concentrates). texts.orig: During crystallization tests, the value of supersaturation ( S -1) remained relatively low and always below 0.15. Takiyama et al. [29] observed, in drowning-out precipitation experiments of NaCl, that heterogeneous nucleation mechanism was predominant for ( S -1) < 0.7. In our system, hydro-dynamically characterized by a laminar regime of the recirculating mother solution (Re ∼ 900), and where unseeded crystallization occurred in presence of a polymeric membrane, primary heterogeneous nucleation was assumed to initiate the crystallization process and to prevail over secondary nucleation. According to classical nucleation theory (CNT), the primary nucleation rate B is related to supersaturation S by the expression:
texts.text: During crystallization tests, the value of supersaturation ( S -1) remained relatively low and always below 0.15. Takiyama et al. [29] observed, in drowning-out precipitation experiments of NaCl, that heterogeneous nucleation mechanism was predominant for ( S -1) < 0.7. In our system, hydro-dynamically characterized by a laminar regime of the recirculating mother solution (Re ∼ 900), and where unseeded crystallization occurred in presence of a polymeric membrane, primary heterogeneous nucleation was assumed to initiate the crystallization process and to prevail over secondary nucleation. According to classical nucleation theory (CNT), the primary nucleation rate B is related to supersaturation S by the expression:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: B = a exp ( -b ln S 2 ) (10.a)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where a is a pre-exponential factor, and b is a constant [30].
texts.text: where a is a pre-exponential factor, and b is a constant [30].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to predictions of CNT, and regardless from brine typology, the number of NaCl crystals increased at higher supersaturation ratios, being in the order of 10 9 -10 11 crystals per cubic meter of solution. The total number of crystals generated from natural brines was average 27% lower than that observed when processing artificial retentate. The logarithm plot of nucleation rate B vs. ln -2 S , reported in Fig. 7, confirmed the good agreement of experimental results with the linear trend expected from equation 10.a. Slopes for NaCl nucleation rates measured in artificial and natural seawater RO concentrates, mathematically corresponding to the term b in Eq. (10.a), differed by 16%. The coefficient b can be expressed as in Gomez-Morales et al. [31]:
texts.text: According to predictions of CNT, and regardless from brine typology, the number of NaCl crystals increased at higher supersaturation ratios, being in the order of 10 9 -10 11 crystals per cubic meter of solution. The total number of crystals generated from natural brines was average 27% lower than that observed when processing artificial retentate. The logarithm plot of nucleation rate B vs. ln -2 S , reported in Fig. 7, confirmed the good agreement of experimental results with the linear trend expected from equation 10.a. Slopes for NaCl nucleation rates measured in artificial and natural seawater RO concentrates, mathematically corresponding to the term b in Eq. (10.a), differed by 16%. The coefficient b can be expressed as in Gomez-Morales et al. [31]:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: b = /RS 16 /EM/ESC 3 /GS 2 3( kBT ) 3 (10.b)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where /ESC is the interfacial energy, /ETB the NaCl molecular volume (1.12 × 10 -29 m 3 = molecular weight/(Avogadro's number × density × number of ions in a formula unit)), kB the Boltzmann's constant and T the absolute temperature. The factor /RS accounts for the reduction of the Gibbs free energy of the system due to heterogeneous nucleation on porous membranes and, according to Curcio et al. [32], depends on the membrane porosity ε and the contact angle /DC2 :
texts.text: where /ESC is the interfacial energy, /ETB the NaCl molecular volume (1.12 × 10 -29 m 3 = molecular weight/(Avogadro's number × density × number of ions in a formula unit)), kB the Boltzmann's constant and T the absolute temperature. The factor /RS accounts for the reduction of the Gibbs free energy of the system due to heterogeneous nucleation on porous membranes and, according to Curcio et al. [32], depends on the membrane porosity ε and the contact angle /DC2 :

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For ε =0.70 and /DC2 =120 ◦ , /RS =0.66. Based on the different slopes of Fig. 7, the values of /ESC calculated using Eq. (10.b) were 9.6 × 10 -3 and 10.2 × 10 -3 J/m 2 for NaCl crystals nucleated from artificial and natural brines, respectively. These results nicely compare with experimental values of interfacial energy in supersaturated solutions for some inorganic salts as reported in Toyokura [33]: 10.5 × 10 -3 J/m 2 for CuSO4 · 5H2O, 8.9 × 10 -3 J/m 2 for K-Alum, 7.3 × 10 -3 J/m 2 for MgSO4 · 7H2O.
texts.text: For ε =0.70 and /DC2 =120 ◦ , /RS =0.66. Based on the different slopes of Fig. 7, the values of /ESC calculated using Eq. (10.b) were 9.6 × 10 -3 and 10.2 × 10 -3 J/m 2 for NaCl crystals nucleated from artificial and natural brines, respectively. These results nicely compare with experimental values of interfacial energy in supersaturated solutions for some inorganic salts as reported in Toyokura [33]: 10.5 × 10 -3 J/m 2 for CuSO4 · 5H2O, 8.9 × 10 -3 J/m 2 for K-Alum, 7.3 × 10 -3 J/m 2 for MgSO4 · 7H2O.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study confirms the ability of membrane distillationcrystallization to concentrate RO brines to achieve a water recovery of greater than 90% with a concomitant reduction in volumetric waste discharged to the environment. Moreover, batch runs carried out on natural RO concentrates resulted in the production of 17kg/m 3 of NaCl crystals, representing the 34% c.a. of the total content of dissolved solids in the brine.
texts.text: This study confirms the ability of membrane distillationcrystallization to concentrate RO brines to achieve a water recovery of greater than 90% with a concomitant reduction in volumetric waste discharged to the environment. Moreover, batch runs carried out on natural RO concentrates resulted in the production of 17kg/m 3 of NaCl crystals, representing the 34% c.a. of the total content of dissolved solids in the brine.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Concentration polarization phenomenon, occurring in proximity of the membrane surface, was limited by operating MDC at moderate transmembrane fluxes (in the order of 10 -4 kgm -2 s -1 ); moreover, the ability to prevent encrustations or irremovable deposits of solids due to uncontrolled supersaturation was recognized as a crucial task in order to achieve long-term stability of the process.
texts.text: Concentration polarization phenomenon, occurring in proximity of the membrane surface, was limited by operating MDC at moderate transmembrane fluxes (in the order of 10 -4 kgm -2 s -1 ); moreover, the ability to prevent encrustations or irremovable deposits of solids due to uncontrolled supersaturation was recognized as a crucial task in order to achieve long-term stability of the process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It was also observed that the relatively low content of organics in natural RO retentate (TOC: 2.1 mg/l), increasing progressively during concentration tests, significantly affects the crystallization kinetics in terms of reduced magma density, nucleation and growth rates, as well as the transmembrane flux. Therefore, adequate pretreatment before RO stage is needed to reduce the negative effect of dissolved organic matter on the MDC performance.
texts.text: It was also observed that the relatively low content of organics in natural RO retentate (TOC: 2.1 mg/l), increasing progressively during concentration tests, significantly affects the crystallization kinetics in terms of reduced magma density, nucleation and growth rates, as well as the transmembrane flux. Therefore, adequate pretreatment before RO stage is needed to reduce the negative effect of dissolved organic matter on the MDC performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In principle, the scale-up of MDC process to industrial scale involving large volumes of brines does not show high technical complexity. With respect to conventional crystallizers, membrane operations are modular, thus resulting in easy to design, high flexibility, advanced level of automation and control at large-scale.
texts.text: In principle, the scale-up of MDC process to industrial scale involving large volumes of brines does not show high technical complexity. With respect to conventional crystallizers, membrane operations are modular, thus resulting in easy to design, high flexibility, advanced level of automation and control at large-scale.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All these data outline the potential impact of MDC on the economical and environmental aspects of membrane desalination industry, and encourage more research efforts in this field.
texts.text: All these data outline the potential impact of MDC on the economical and environmental aspects of membrane desalination industry, and encourage more research efforts in this field.
